tossups.question: Description acceptable. Kevin Kiernan used UV light to study this object, which was first described by Franciscus Junius. This object is the subject of Andy Orchard’s book Pride and Prodigies. Kemp Malone improved Nikolaj Grundtvig’s reconstructions of text from the edges of this object. The first writer of this document stopped in the middle of a sentence and was replaced by a scribe who spelled a name in it with an “I” instead of an “E.” This document includes a letter from Alexander the Great to Aristotle and a bestiary called the Wonders of the East. The (*) Ashburnham House fire scorched this object, which contains the poem Judith. This document, which was most likely created around the early 11th century, is often named after its first owner, Laurence Nowell. This document’s central section contains characters like Onela, Heardred, Hygelac, and Wiglaf. For 10 points, identify this source of the longest poem in Old English.
tossups.answer: the Beowulf manuscript [accept similar answers; accept Nowell Codex before “Laurence Nowell”; accept Cotton MS Vitellius A.xv; prompt on answers indicating a poem rather than the physical document]
tossups.answer_sanitized: the Beowulf manuscript [accept similar answers; accept Nowell Codex before "Laurence Nowell"; accept Cotton MS Vitellius A.xv; prompt on answers indicating a poem rather than the physical document]

This object is the subject of Andy Orchard's book Pride and Prodigies. Kemp Malone improved Nikolaj Grundtvig's reconstructions of text from the edges of this object. The first writer of this document stopped in the middle of a sentence and was replaced by a scribe who spelled a name in it with an "I" instead of an "E." This document includes a letter from Alexander the Great to Aristotle and a bestiary called the Wonders of the East. The (*) Ashburnham House fire scorched this object, which contains the poem Judith. This document, which was most likely created around the early 11th century, is often named after its first owner, Laurence Nowell. This document's central section contains characters like Onela, Heardred, Hygelac, and Wiglaf. For 10 points, identify this source of the longest poem in Old English.

bonuses.leadin: This collection contains a fragmentary 9th-century poem about a “strong servant of the Savior,” Judith. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Old English manuscript, which depicts fantastic peoples such as blemmyae and hostes in the illustrated Wonders of the East. Grímur Jónsson Thorkelin transcribed Beowulf from this text.

This Old English elegy from the Exeter Book begins with the compound uhtcearu (“OOT-cha-roo”), meaning “pre-dawn-sorrow.” Its speaker predicts, “That man of mine / will drag his days under a mighty mind-caring.”

This Beowulf character is described by the similar compound term uhtfloga (“OOT-flog-uh”), meaning “dawn-flyer.” At the end of the poem, an aged Beowulf slays this character with assistance from Wiglaf.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This collection contains a fragmentary 9th-century poem about a "strong servant of the Savior," Judith. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this Old English manuscript, which depicts fantastic peoples such as blemmyae and hostes in the illustrated Wonders of the East. Grimur Jonsson Thorkelin transcribed Beowulf from this text.

This Old English elegy from the Exeter Book begins with the compound uhtcearu ("OOT-cha-roo"), meaning "pre-dawn-sorrow." Its speaker predicts, "That man of mine / will drag his days under a mighty mind-caring."

This Beowulf character is described by the similar compound term uhtfloga ("OOT-flog-uh"), meaning "dawn-flyer." At the end of the poem, an aged Beowulf slays this character with assistance from Wiglaf.

bonuses.leadin: Objects in this text include the not-very-useful sword Hrunting, the somewhat-more-useful Nægling, and the mysterious “glof” belonging to the first of its three monstrous protagonists. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Old English epic, in which the title warrior saves Hrothgar from the depredations of Grendel.

The only surviving text of Beowulf is in this manuscript, the second part of Cotton Vitellius A.xv. [“A-fifteen”]. It has been suggested that heavy wear on one page of this manuscript, held at the British Library, is the result of readers’ interest in the dragon.
There may be some connection between a song sung in Hrothgar’s hall and the Old English text of this kind often named for Caedmon, which begins “Now we must honour the guardian of heaven.”
